abstract:
This book examines the surprisingly significant role played by "weak" presidents in European politics—those who, despite being directly or indirectly elected, hold limited constitutional weight. By distinguishing these figures from their stronger counterparts through an evaluation of their formal legal prerogatives, the study investigates six Western European nations. These include countries with consistent constitutional frameworks—Germany and Italy, where weak presidents are indirectly elected—and those with "inconsistent" designs: Austria, Ireland, Finland, and Portugal, where weak presidents are chosen by popular vote.

Moving beyond traditional comparative literature that focuses primarily on formal executive authority, this work shifts the spotlight to the strategic use of informal powers. It introduces a comprehensive typology and definition of informal authority, followed by an empirical investigation into presidential activism that occurs outside of strict constitutional mandates. To explain when and why these informal interventions succeed, the book adopts an interactionist approach, blending presidency-centered structural analysis with president-centered individual agency. Ultimately, the book argues that while the political "opportunity structure" provides the opening for action, it is the president’s level of public support that serves as the decisive factor in their political influence.
